questions.question_text: A buyer's broker receives a bonus check directly from a home builder at closing. Is this permissible under Oregon law?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation must go through the principal broker
distractors: Yes, if all parties provide written consent
Yes, if the amount is disclosed at closing
Only with the principal broker's verbal approval
questions.explanation: Oregon law requires that all compensation to brokers be paid through their principal broker. A broker cannot receive compensation directly from any party other than their own principal broker.

questions.question_text: A broker left Brokerage A and joined Brokerage B. A commission from a transaction she started at Brokerage A is now due. Who may pay her?
questions.correct_answer: Her former principal broker at Brokerage A
distractors: Her new principal broker at Brokerage B
Either principal broker at her choice
questions.explanation: Oregon allows a former principal broker to pay compensation for transactions that began during the broker's employment at that brokerage. The new principal broker at Brokerage B was not involved in the original transaction.

questions.question_text: What document must exist between a principal broker and each affiliated broker in Oregon?
questions.explanation: Oregon requires a written employment agreement between the principal broker and each broker. This agreement outlines the terms of compensation, supervision, and whether the broker is an employee or independent contractor.

questions.question_text: Under 2026 Oregon law, a broker wants to donate part of her commission to a local housing nonprofit. Which type of organization qualifies to receive shared compensation?
questions.explanation: As of 2026, Oregon licensees may share compensation with 501(c)(3) charities. This is a specific exception to the general rule that brokers may only receive compensation through their principal broker.

questions.question_text: A principal broker classifies all affiliated brokers as independent contractors but sets their daily office hours and requires attendance at weekly meetings. What risk does this create?
questions.explanation: When a principal broker exercises significant control over how and when brokers work, the IRS may reclassify those brokers as employees regardless of the written agreement. This triggers payroll tax obligations and potential penalties.

questions.question_text: What must every Oregon brokerage register with OREA before conducting business?
distractors: A DBA with the Secretary of State only
questions.explanation: Every Oregon brokerage must operate under a Registered Business Name (RBN) that is registered with the Oregon Real Estate Agency. This ensures the public can identify licensed brokerages.

questions.question_text: A brokerage runs a social media ad featuring property listings but omits its RBN. What Oregon advertising requirement has been violated?
questions.correct_answer: The RBN must appear in all advertising
distractors: The principal broker's license number is required
Only print ads require the RBN
Social media ads are exempt from RBN rules
questions.explanation: Oregon requires that the brokerage's Registered Business Name be displayed in all advertising, including social media. There is no exemption for digital or online platforms.

questions.question_text: Under 2026 Oregon law, how many managing principal brokers is each RBN required to have?
questions.correct_answer: One managing principal broker per RBN
distractors: One for every ten affiliated brokers
None if the RBN has fewer than five brokers
questions.explanation: As of 2026, each Registered Business Name must have one managing principal broker who is ultimately responsible for the brokerage's operations, compliance, and supervision of all affiliated brokers.

questions.question_text: A managing principal broker oversees two separate RBNs. She discovers a trust account violation at one location. Under 2026 law, who bears ultimate responsibility?
questions.correct_answer: The managing principal broker of that RBN
distractors: All brokers affiliated with both RBNs
Only the broker who handled the funds
questions.explanation: Under 2026 Oregon law, each RBN has one managing principal broker who is ultimately responsible for all operations under that RBN. This includes trust account compliance and broker supervision at that business.

questions.question_text: Where must an Oregon brokerage physically display its RBN?
distractors: Only on the principal broker's license
On each broker's business card only
questions.explanation: Oregon requires that the Registered Business Name be displayed at the brokerage's place of business. This ensures consumers visiting the office can verify they are dealing with a properly registered brokerage.

questions.question_text: Under 2026 HB 3137, a group of brokers wants to form a team within their brokerage. Whose approval is required?
distractors: A majority vote of all affiliated brokers
questions.explanation: Under 2026 HB 3137, real estate teams are a subdivision of the RBN and require the approval of the managing principal broker. Teams cannot operate independently of the brokerage structure.

questions.question_text: A newly formed Oregon team wants to call itself 'Pacific Realty Group.' Does this name comply with 2026 team naming rules?
distractors: Yes, as long as the RBN is also displayed
No, team names must match the RBN exactly
questions.explanation: Under 2026 HB 3137, Oregon team names cannot include the words 'realty' or 'real estate.' This restriction prevents consumer confusion between a team and a standalone brokerage.

questions.question_text: When must an Oregon team disclose its team status to a prospective client under 2026 law?
distractors: At the time an offer is submitted
questions.explanation: Under 2026 HB 3137, teams must disclose their team status to prospective clients before entering into representation agreements. This ensures consumers understand they are working with a team rather than the brokerage as a whole.

questions.question_text: A managing principal broker establishes verbal supervisory guidelines for the teams under her RBN. Does this satisfy 2026 Oregon requirements?
questions.correct_answer: No, written compliance supervision systems are required
distractors: Yes, if all team members acknowledge them
Yes, verbal policies are sufficient for small teams
Only if filed with OREA annually
questions.explanation: Under 2026 HB 3137, managing principal brokers must establish written compliance supervision systems for teams. Verbal guidelines alone do not satisfy the statutory requirement for documented oversight procedures.

questions.question_text: A managing principal broker is drafting supervisory agreements for her teams. Under 2026 HB 3137, what additional planning element must these agreements include?
questions.explanation: Under 2026 HB 3137, written supervisory agreements for teams must include succession plans. This ensures continuity of client service and compliance oversight if the team leader or managing principal broker is no longer available.
